rationale: The citing paper uses Bayer, Tuli, and Skiera (2017) in its core conceptual setup, specifically to support the claim that firms do not consistently disclose forward-looking customer satisfaction information. That claim is tied to the paper's MAO-based argument that short sellers can obtain an information advantage from private customer-satisfaction data, which is central to the proposed mechanism. The citation is also used again in the discussion to justify disclosure-related regulatory implications. Because it informs key framing and interpretation rather than serving as a passing literature-list mention, the role is substantive.
relation_description: The citing study relies on Bayer et al. (2017) as empirical background for limited customer-metric disclosure, which supports its information-asymmetry premise about short sellers. It then leverages that prior disclosure work when drawing implications about mandating customer-metric reporting.
